subject: public_law
topic_heading: Duty to Consult and Accommodate — Haida Nation Framework
subtopic_heading: Duty to Consult — Trigger and Spectrum
fact_pattern: The British Columbia government issues a permit to a logging company to harvest timber in an area where the Haida Nation has a pending but unproven claim to Aboriginal title. The Haida Nation has not yet established its title in court. The Haida seek a declaration that the Crown failed to consult them before issuing the logging permit.
call_of_question: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia, what triggers the Crown's duty to consult Indigenous peoples, and does the duty arise even before Aboriginal rights or title have been proven?

options.A: The duty to consult arises only after Aboriginal title has been legally established by a court; until then, the Crown has no obligation to consult.
options.B: The duty to consult arises where: (1) the Crown has actual or constructive knowledge of a potential Aboriginal claim or right; and (2) the Crown contemplates conduct that might adversely affect the right or claim. The duty arises before rights are proven; the strength of the claim affects the depth of the duty.
options.C: The duty to consult is owed only to First Nations that have signed treaties; non-treaty groups like the Haida have no such right.
options.D: The duty to consult arises only when the Crown proposes legislation that would directly affect Aboriginal peoples, not when issuing administrative permits.

explanation: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, In Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the Supreme Court of Canada established that the Crown's duty to consult and accommodate is triggered when two conditions are met: (1) the Crown has actual or constructive knowledge of the potential existence of an Aboriginal right or title claim; and (2) the Crown contemplates conduct — including administrative decisions like permit issuance — that might adversely affect that right or claim. Critically, the duty arises before rights are proven; the justification is the honour of the Crown and the need to protect Indigenous interests during the period of uncertainty. The scope of the duty (deep or shallow) is calibrated to the strength of the claim.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to consult arises before rights are proven, not after; requiring proof before consultation would effectively defeat the purpose of the doctrine by allowing the Crown to act before rights are established.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The duty to consult arises where: (1) the Crown has actual or constructive knowledge of a potential Aboriginal claim or right; and (2) the Crown contemplates conduct that might adversely affect the right or claim. The duty arises before rights are proven; the strength of the claim affects the depth of the duty.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to consult applies to all First Nations and Indigenous peoples with potential claims or rights, regardless of whether they have signed treaties; Haida Nation itself involved a non-treaty nation.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to consult extends to administrative decisions including permit and licence issuance, not only to legislation; the Crown's executive actions triggering the duty include regulatory approvals that may adversely affect Aboriginal claims.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Duty to Consult — Trigger and Spectrum
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73; Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35

tested_concepts: Haida Nation two-part trigger for duty to consult
duty arises before Aboriginal rights proven
spectrum of consultation calibrated to claim strength

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Duty to Consult and Accommodate — Haida Nation Framework
subtopic_heading: Depth of the Duty to Consult — Spectrum and Accommodation
fact_pattern: An Ontario First Nation has a strong and well-documented claim to Aboriginal title over a large land area. The provincial government proposes to approve a major mineral development project in the heart of that claimed territory. The project would cause significant and potentially irreversible disruption to the land. The government consults with the First Nation but provides only two weeks to respond and does not modify the project to address concerns raised.
call_of_question: Under the Haida Nation spectrum, how deep is the duty to consult and accommodate in circumstances involving a strong claim and potentially significant adverse effects?

options.A: The depth of the duty is always the same regardless of the strength of the claim or the severity of the potential impact; all consultations require a two-week response period.
options.B: Where the claim is strong and the potential adverse effects are significant, the duty to consult sits at the deeper end of the spectrum, requiring meaningful engagement — including a reasonable opportunity to understand and respond to the proposal — and may require accommodation such as altering or delaying the project to address concerns. A two-week consultation period for a major irreversible project may fall short.
options.C: Where Aboriginal title has not been established, the Crown has only a minimal duty to inform the First Nation of the proposed project; no accommodation is required.
options.D: The duty to accommodate means the Crown must obtain the First Nation's veto over any project that affects claimed territory.

explanation: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, In Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the Supreme Court explained that the duty to consult and accommodate exists on a spectrum. At the lower end (weak claim, minor impact), the Crown may satisfy its duty by providing notice and basic information. At the deeper end (strong claim, significant impact), the duty requires meaningful engagement — including a genuine opportunity to understand and respond to the Crown's proposal — and accommodation: modifying the Crown's proposed conduct to address the Aboriginal group's concerns as far as possible without undue hardship. A major, irreversible project in the heart of a strongly supported claimed title area requires deep consultation and likely some form of accommodation.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The duty is not uniform across all cases; the depth varies along a spectrum calibrated to the strength of the claim and the severity of the potential impact.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Where the claim is strong and the potential adverse effects are significant, the duty to consult sits at the deeper end of the spectrum, requiring meaningful engagement — including a reasonable opportunity to understand and respond to the proposal — and may require accommodation such as altering or delaying the project to address concerns. A two-week consultation period for a major irreversible project may fall short.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Even where title has not been legally established, a strong and well-documented claim combined with significant potential adverse effects calls for deep consultation and accommodation, not merely notification.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to accommodate does not confer a veto; the Crown must engage meaningfully and accommodate concerns as far as possible, but ultimate decision-making authority remains with the Crown subject to the justification test for infringement of proven rights.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Depth of the Duty to Consult — Spectrum and Accommodation
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73; Taku River Tlingit First Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 74; Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35

tested_concepts: Haida Nation spectrum deep consultation
accommodation requirement for strong claims
no veto but genuine engagement required

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Section 35 Constitution Act 1982 — Existing Aboriginal Rights
subtopic_heading: Section 35 — Sparrow Justification Test for Infringement of Aboriginal Rights
fact_pattern: The Musqueam Nation has an established Aboriginal right to fish for food under s. 35 of the Constitution Act, 1982. The federal government enacts a regulation limiting the Musqueam's net length during certain spawning periods to protect the fish stock. The Musqueam challenge the regulation as an unjustified infringement of their s. 35 right.
call_of_question: Under R. v Sparrow, what two-part test must the Crown satisfy to justify a regulation that infringes a proven Aboriginal right under s. 35?

options.A: The Crown must prove: (1) the regulation pursues a pressing and substantial objective; and (2) the regulation is proportionate — rationally connected, minimally impairing, and with proportionate effects — under s. 1 of the Charter.
options.B: The Crown must prove: (1) the infringement is justified by a compelling and substantial legislative objective (such as conservation); and (2) the infringement is consistent with the fiduciary duty the Crown owes to Aboriginal peoples, which includes giving Aboriginal rights priority after conservation needs are met.
options.C: Once the Aboriginal right is proven, the Crown cannot justify any infringement; all regulations affecting s. 35 rights are unconstitutional.
options.D: The Crown must only prove that the regulation applies equally to all Canadians; equal application is a complete justification for any infringement of s. 35 rights.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, In R. v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, the Supreme Court of Canada established the two-part justification framework for s. 35 infringements. First, the Crown must identify a compelling and substantial legislative objective — conservation and management of a natural resource is a valid objective. Second, the Crown's action must be consistent with its fiduciary duty to Aboriginal peoples, which requires, among other things, giving priority to Aboriginal rights after conservation needs are met, minimizing the infringement, and consulting the affected group. This Sparrow justification test is distinct from the Charter s. 1 analysis.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The s. 1 Charter justification test (Oakes) does not apply to s. 35 rights; s. 35 rights are not subject to the Charter. The Sparrow two-part test applies separately to s. 35 infringement justification.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Crown must prove: (1) the infringement is justified by a compelling and substantial legislative objective (such as conservation); and (2) the infringement is consistent with the fiduciary duty the Crown owes to Aboriginal peoples, which includes giving Aboriginal rights priority after conservation needs are met.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Section 35 rights are not absolute; the Crown may justify an infringement of s. 35 rights by satisfying the Sparrow justification test.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Equal application of a regulation to all Canadians does not justify infringement of s. 35 rights; equal application may actually constitute adverse discrimination against Aboriginal rights by failing to give priority to those rights.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Section 35 — Sparrow Justification Test for Infringement of Aboriginal Rights
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075

tested_concepts: Sparrow two-part s. 35 justification test
compelling and substantial objective
fiduciary duty and Aboriginal priority

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Reconciliation — Honour of the Crown and UNDRIP
subtopic_heading: Reconciliation Principle — Honour of the Crown and UNDRIP
fact_pattern: A law student argues to her colleague that the legal concept of the 'honour of the Crown' has no practical legal effect — it is merely a rhetorical flourish that courts use to signal good intentions toward Indigenous peoples without imposing any enforceable obligations.
call_of_question: Is the 'honour of the Crown' principle merely rhetorical, or does it impose legally enforceable obligations in the Crown-Indigenous relationship?

options.A: The honour of the Crown is merely a political and moral concept; it creates no legally enforceable obligations and cannot ground court orders.
options.B: The honour of the Crown is a foundational constitutional principle that has been recognized by the Supreme Court of Canada as imposing enforceable obligations on the Crown; it underpins the duty to consult and accommodate, the fiduciary duty, and the requirement that Crown conduct must not be interpreted to allow the Crown to take unconscionable advantage of its Aboriginal partners.
options.C: The honour of the Crown is a concept that applies only after Aboriginal rights have been fully negotiated through treaty; it has no application during the pre-treaty or litigation phase.
options.D: The honour of the Crown was abolished by the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples (UNDRIP), which replaced it with the concept of free, prior, and informed consent.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, the Supreme Court of Canada has consistently held that the honour of the Crown is a foundational constitutional principle with real legal effect. In Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, the Court held that the honour of the Crown underpins the duty to consult and accommodate. In R. v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, it informed the fiduciary duty and justification analysis. The principle requires the Crown to act with integrity in all dealings with Indigenous peoples and prohibits the Crown from acting in ways that would take unconscionable advantage of Aboriginal peoples. UNDRIP (adopted by Canada in 2016 and incorporated into Canadian law through legislation) does not replace the honour of the Crown but complements it by affirming the principle of free, prior, and informed consent as a further standard for Crown-Indigenous engagement.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The Supreme Court of Canada has specifically held that the honour of the Crown is not merely rhetorical; it imposes legally enforceable obligations on the Crown and can ground court orders including orders to consult.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The honour of the Crown is a foundational constitutional principle that has been recognized by the Supreme Court of Canada as imposing enforceable obligations on the Crown; it underpins the duty to consult and accommodate, the fiduciary duty, and the requirement that Crown conduct must not be interpreted to allow the Crown to take unconscionable advantage of its Aboriginal partners.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The honour of the Crown operates throughout all phases of the Crown-Indigenous relationship, including before treaties are negotiated, during litigation over claimed rights, and in the implementation of treaty rights.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. UNDRIP complements and reinforces the honour of the Crown principle; it did not abolish it. Free, prior, and informed consent under UNDRIP coexists with the honour of the Crown as a standard informing Crown-Indigenous relations.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Reconciliation Principle — Honour of the Crown and UNDRIP
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075

tested_concepts: honour of the Crown legally enforceable principle
underpins duty to consult and fiduciary duty
UNDRIP complements honour of the Crown

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: Duty to Consult — When Duty Is Triggered
fact_pattern: A provincial government is considering approval of a new mining project in an area where a First Nation has filed but not yet proven a claim to Aboriginal title. The project would involve road construction through the claimed territory. The province argues the duty to consult does not arise because the Aboriginal title claim has not been proven in court and s. 35 rights have not been established.
call_of_question: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, when is the Crown's duty to consult triggered?

options.A: The duty to consult arises only after Aboriginal rights or title have been formally established by a court judgment; unproven claims do not trigger any duty.
options.B: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the duty to consult is triggered when the Crown has: (1) actual or constructive knowledge of the potential existence of an Aboriginal right or title; (2) contemplates conduct (Crown action or decision); and (3) that conduct might adversely affect that right or title. Proof of the right or title in court is not required; a credible, unresolved claim is sufficient to trigger the duty.
options.C: The duty to consult applies only to decisions made under federal legislation; provincial resource management decisions never trigger the duty.
options.D: The duty to consult is triggered only where the First Nation has filed a Statement of Claim in court asserting Aboriginal title; administrative claims or notices to government are insufficient.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, In Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the Supreme Court established that the duty to consult is triggered at a low threshold — not proof of Aboriginal rights or title, but rather the Crown's knowledge (actual or constructive) that a credible claim exists, combined with contemplated Crown action that might adversely affect the claimed rights or title. The rationale is the honour of the Crown and the practical reality that waiting for court-established rights before consulting would leave Aboriginal peoples unprotected during the often-lengthy rights litigation process. The province's argument that only proven rights attract consultation is squarely rejected by Haida Nation. A filed (though not yet proven) claim to Aboriginal title, combined with a resource project in the claimed territory, clearly triggers the duty.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Haida Nation directly and explicitly rejected the argument that court-established rights are a prerequisite to the duty to consult. The duty arises from credible, unproven claims and is not delayed until rights are established in court.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73, the duty to consult is triggered when the Crown has: (1) actual or constructive knowledge of the potential existence of an Aboriginal right or title; (2) contemplates conduct (Crown action or decision); and (3) that conduct might adversely affect that right or title. Proof of the right or title in court is not required; a credible, unresolved claim is sufficient to trigger the duty.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to consult applies to both federal and provincial Crown action. Haida Nation itself was a challenge to a provincial (British Columbia) forest management decision, confirming that provincial resource management decisions can and do trigger the duty.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Filing a Statement of Claim in court is not a prerequisite to triggering the duty to consult. The duty is triggered by the Crown's knowledge of a credible claim to Aboriginal rights or title; such knowledge can come from administrative filings, notice to government, publicly known claims, or the Crown's own knowledge of traditional land use.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Duty to Consult — When Duty Is Triggered
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73

tested_concepts: Haida Nation three-part trigger for duty to consult
proof of rights not required — credible claim sufficient
provincial Crown subject to duty to consult

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Duty to Consult — Depth of Consultation
subtopic_heading: Duty to Consult — Spectrum of Depth and Strength of Claim
fact_pattern: A First Nation holds a strong treaty right protecting its fishing grounds from development. The provincial government proposes to issue a permit for an industrial facility that would discharge treated wastewater into the river system at the heart of the treaty fishing grounds, significantly affecting fish populations. The province offers to notify the First Nation and accept written submissions, but does not provide meaningful opportunity for the First Nation to influence the permit conditions.
call_of_question: Under the Haida Nation spectrum of consultation, was the province's approach to consultation adequate in these circumstances?

options.A: Notification and written submissions are always sufficient consultation regardless of the strength of the claim or the severity of the impact; deeper consultation is only required where the First Nation has a court order.
options.B: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, the depth of consultation required sits on a spectrum calibrated to the strength of the Aboriginal right or claim and the severity of the potential impact on that right. Where a strong, established treaty right is at significant risk from proposed Crown action, the Crown must engage in deep consultation — which goes beyond mere notification and receipt of written submissions to include meaningful dialogue, genuine consideration of the First Nation's concerns, and accommodation where appropriate. Notification and written submissions alone are inadequate where the treaty right is strong and the impact serious.
options.C: The duty to consult is satisfied whenever the Crown informs a First Nation of a proposed action in advance; what the First Nation does with that notification is their responsibility.
options.D: Deep consultation (as opposed to minimal notice) is only required for decisions that would permanently extinguish an Aboriginal right; a temporary wastewater discharge permit does not require more than notification.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, established that the duty to consult exists on a spectrum: at the low end (weak or speculative claims, minor impacts), notice and an opportunity to respond may suffice; at the high end (strong, established rights, significant adverse impacts), deep consultation is required, including meaningful dialogue, genuine consideration of the First Nation's concerns, and potentially accommodation. An established treaty right protecting fishing grounds is a strong, recognized right at the top of the spectrum. A proposed wastewater discharge that would significantly affect fish populations is a potentially severe adverse impact on that right. This combination places the consultation obligation at the high end of the spectrum — notification and written submissions alone are plainly inadequate. The province must engage in genuine dialogue, consider the First Nation's concerns in setting permit conditions, and accommodate where feasible.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Notification and written submissions are not universally sufficient. Haida Nation's spectrum approach calibrates the depth of consultation to the strength of the claim and severity of the impact. For strong rights and serious impacts, much deeper consultation is required.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, the depth of consultation required sits on a spectrum calibrated to the strength of the Aboriginal right or claim and the severity of the potential impact on that right. Where a strong, established treaty right is at significant risk from proposed Crown action, the Crown must engage in deep consultation — which goes beyond mere notification and receipt of written submissions to include meaningful dialogue, genuine consideration of the First Nation's concerns, and accommodation where appropriate. Notification and written submissions alone are inadequate where the treaty right is strong and the impact serious.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to consult is not discharged merely by informing the First Nation of a proposed action. Haida Nation requires meaningful consultation appropriate to the circumstances, not a unilateral notification that shifts responsibility to the First Nation.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Deep consultation is not limited to decisions permanently extinguishing Aboriginal rights. Haida Nation's spectrum is based on strength of the right and severity of the impact; a significant adverse impact on an established treaty right (even from a temporary permit) can require deep consultation.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Duty to Consult — Spectrum of Depth and Strength of Claim
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73

tested_concepts: Haida Nation spectrum — depth calibrated to strength of claim and impact
notification insufficient for strong rights and serious impacts
deep consultation requirements

subject: public_law
topic_heading: Duty to Accommodate — Content and Limits
subtopic_heading: Duty to Accommodate — What Accommodation Requires
fact_pattern: Following deep consultation with a First Nation regarding a proposed pipeline, the Crown acknowledges that the project will adversely affect the First Nation's s. 35 rights. The First Nation demands a veto over the project — arguing that the duty to accommodate requires the Crown to obtain the First Nation's free, prior, and informed consent before proceeding. The Crown argues consultation is complete and it can approve the project over the First Nation's objection.
call_of_question: Under Haida Nation and subsequent case law, does the duty to accommodate include a First Nation veto over Crown decisions affecting their s. 35 rights?

options.A: Yes — where the duty to consult is triggered and consultation has occurred, the First Nation has an absolute veto over any Crown decision that would adversely affect their s. 35 rights.
options.B: Under Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, the duty to consult and accommodate does not give First Nations a veto over Crown decisions. Accommodation requires the Crown to genuinely consider the First Nation's concerns and make reasonable adjustments to minimize adverse impacts, but it does not give First Nations the right to block government decisions entirely. The Crown may proceed with a project over a First Nation's objection if it has fulfilled its consultation obligations and provided appropriate accommodation, though courts will scrutinize whether the accommodation was adequate. A veto would only be available for decisions affecting established core Aboriginal rights in extreme cases.
options.C: The duty to accommodate requires the Crown to obtain the First Nation's written consent before proceeding; unanimous consent from all members of the First Nation is required.
options.D: Once the consultation process is complete and the Crown has accepted written submissions, the accommodation obligation is automatically discharged; no further steps are required regardless of the adequacy of the consultation.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, Haida Nation expressly addresses and rejects the concept of a First Nation veto over Crown resource decisions. The Court held that the duty to consult and, where appropriate, accommodate does not give Aboriginal peoples a veto over Crown decisions; it is a process right, not an outcome right. Accommodation requires the Crown to genuinely consider the First Nation's concerns and make reasonable adjustments — route modifications, environmental conditions, revenue sharing, monitoring requirements — but does not require the Crown to obtain consent or abandon projects. This remains the law even under the principle of free, prior, and informed consent in UNDRIP; Canadian courts have not interpreted UNDRIP as creating a legal veto in domestic law. That said, where accommodation is grossly inadequate, courts may grant judicial review to require additional consultation and accommodation before the project proceeds.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Haida Nation explicitly stated that the duty to consult and accommodate does not give First Nations a veto over Crown decisions. The duty is one of process — genuine consultation and reasonable accommodation — not an outcome guarantee.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Haida Nation v British Columbia, 2004 SCC 73, the duty to consult and accommodate does not give First Nations a veto over Crown decisions. Accommodation requires the Crown to genuinely consider the First Nation's concerns and make reasonable adjustments to minimize adverse impacts, but it does not give First Nations the right to block government decisions entirely. The Crown may proceed with a project over a First Nation's objection if it has fulfilled its consultation obligations and provided appropriate accommodation, though courts will scrutinize whether the accommodation was adequate. A veto would only be available for decisions affecting established core Aboriginal rights in extreme cases.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Canadian law does not require written consent from all members of a First Nation as a condition of Crown decision-making. The duty is to consult and accommodate, not to obtain consent. UNDRIP's free, prior, and informed consent standard has not been interpreted in Canadian law as a legal veto requiring unanimous membership consent.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Receipt of written submissions does not automatically discharge the accommodation obligation. Accommodation requires the Crown to genuinely consider the submissions and make reasonable adjustments where feasible. A formalistic process that accepts submissions but disregards them does not satisfy the duty.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Duty to Accommodate — What Accommodation Requires
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; Haida Nation v British Columbia (Minister of Forests), 2004 SCC 73

tested_concepts: no veto for First Nations under duty to accommodate
accommodation as reasonable adjustment not consent requirement
UNDRIP not creating legal veto in Canadian law

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: Sparrow Justification — Crown Burden and Priority
fact_pattern: The federal government enacts a fisheries regulation that limits the annual salmon catch of a First Nation whose members have exercised an Aboriginal fishing right for generations. The regulation prioritizes commercial fishing licences over the First Nation's food fishing right. The First Nation challenges the regulation as an unjustified infringement of their s. 35 Aboriginal right. The Crown argues the regulation is justified because conservation of salmon is a valid legislative objective.
call_of_question: Under the Sparrow justification framework, which of the following MOST accurately states the Crown's burden once an infringement of a s. 35 right has been established?

options.A: Once an infringement is established, the Crown must prove the regulation promotes conservation; no additional priority requirement applies, and commercial fishing interests may be prioritized over Aboriginal food fishing.
options.B: Under R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, once infringement of a s. 35 right is established, the Crown bears the burden of justifying the infringement by showing: (1) the infringement is in furtherance of a valid legislative objective (e.g., conservation, resource management, protection of public safety — though objectives must be compelling and substantial); and (2) the infringement upholds the honour of the Crown, which includes the requirement that Aboriginal rights be given priority over other user groups. Prioritizing commercial fishing licences over Aboriginal food fishing rights would undermine the priority requirement and would not justify the infringement under Sparrow.
options.C: Under Sparrow, the Crown is not required to demonstrate any priority for Aboriginal rights; it need only show the regulation serves a valid public interest, and courts will defer to the Crown's resource management choices.
options.D: The Sparrow justification test mirrors the s. 1 Oakes test; the Crown must show the regulation is prescribed by law and proportional to the government's objective. Conservation is always a proportionate objective for fisheries regulations.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, established a two-stage justification test for Crown infringement of s. 35 rights. Stage 1 asks whether the Crown has a valid legislative objective — the Court was clear that not all objectives will qualify; the objective must be compelling and substantial (conservation is a recognized valid objective, but mere economic convenience is not). Stage 2 requires the Crown to satisfy the honour of the Crown, which includes the critical requirement that Aboriginal rights be given priority over other user groups in resource allocation. This is the exam trap: many candidates know 'conservation' is a valid objective and assume it automatically justifies the infringement. But Sparrow's priority requirement means that even where conservation is the objective, Aboriginal food fishing must be given priority in the allocation of the conservation-limited resource over commercial fishing interests. A regulation that gives priority to commercial licences over Aboriginal food fishing fails at Stage 2 of Sparrow, regardless of the validity of the conservation objective.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Conservation alone does not justify the infringement. Sparrow's Stage 2 (honour of the Crown) requires that Aboriginal rights be given priority over other user groups in the allocation of the resource. Prioritizing commercial licences over Aboriginal food fishing rights fails this requirement.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075, once infringement of a s. 35 right is established, the Crown bears the burden of justifying the infringement by showing: (1) the infringement is in furtherance of a valid legislative objective (e.g., conservation, resource management, protection of public safety — though objectives must be compelling and substantial); and (2) the infringement upholds the honour of the Crown, which includes the requirement that Aboriginal rights be given priority over other user groups. Prioritizing commercial fishing licences over Aboriginal food fishing rights would undermine the priority requirement and would not justify the infringement under Sparrow.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Sparrow rejects blanket deference to Crown resource management choices. The justification test imposes real substantive requirements, including the priority requirement at Stage 2. Courts do not simply defer to the Crown's allocation decisions.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The Sparrow justification test is not identical to the Oakes s. 1 test. While both require valid objectives, Sparrow has its own tailored elements — including the priority requirement — that are distinct from Oakes. Conservation is a recognized valid objective under Sparrow but it does not automatically satisfy the full justification analysis.

source_reference.subject: Public Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 9 — Indigenous Law and Crown Obligations
source_reference.heading: Sparrow Justification — Crown Burden and Priority
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35; R v Sparrow, [1990] 1 SCR 1075

tested_concepts: Sparrow two-stage justification test
priority requirement — Aboriginal rights over other user groups
conservation as valid objective but not sufficient alone

subject: public_law
subtopic_heading: Aboriginal Title — Tsilhqot'in Nation Test and Consequences
fact_pattern: The Tsilhqot'in Nation seeks a declaration of Aboriginal title over a large area of British Columbia land that was used by their ancestors in a semi-nomadic pattern — traversing the territory for hunting, fishing, and seasonal settlement. The Crown argues Aboriginal title can only be established over specific sites of intensive, regular, and exclusive use; vast tracts of land used in a mobile pattern cannot be the subject of Aboriginal title.
call_of_question: Under Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, what is the test for establishing Aboriginal title over territory, and was the Crown's narrow interpretation of the test correct?

options.A: Aboriginal title can only be established over specific sites of intensive regular use; the Crown's narrow site-specific approach is correct and semi-nomadic use of vast tracts cannot ground Aboriginal title.
options.B: Under Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, Aboriginal title is established over territory (not merely specific sites) where the claimant can demonstrate: (1) sufficient occupation — whether intensive or through semi-nomadic patterns of use — prior to Crown sovereignty; (2) continuity of occupation between pre-sovereignty use and present-day rights; and (3) exclusive occupation, meaning the group treated the land as its own and excluded others. The Court rejected the Crown's site-specific approach: semi-nomadic, territorial use of vast tracts of land can establish title over the entire territory, not just the specific sites of intensive use. Once title is established, the Crown must justify any infringement under the Sparrow framework.
options.C: Aboriginal title in Canada is limited to lands specifically identified in treaties; lands not covered by treaties cannot be the subject of an Aboriginal title claim in court.
options.D: Tsilhqot'in Nation established that Aboriginal title gives First Nations absolute ownership of land that cannot be infringed by the Crown under any circumstances; the Sparrow justification test does not apply to Aboriginal title lands.

explanation: Under Constitution Act, 1982, s. 35, Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, was a landmark decision granting the first judicial declaration of Aboriginal title over a vast territory in Canadian history. The Court held that the test for Aboriginal title requires: (1) Sufficient occupation — the land must have been used, controlled, or occupied by the claimant's ancestors prior to Crown sovereignty; this does not require intensive, permanent settlement at specific sites; semi-nomadic patterns of territorial use (hunting, fishing, traveling through) are sufficient if the group exercised regular and exclusive control over the land; (2) Continuity — a connection between historic occupation and present-day rights must be shown; and (3) Exclusivity — the group must have treated the land as its own and had the intention and capacity to exclude others. Once Aboriginal title is established, the Crown must justify any infringement under a modified Sparrow framework: the objective must be compelling and substantial, the infringement must be consistent with the Crown's fiduciary duty to the First Nation, and the First Nation must be compensated for interference with its title land.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The Tsilhqot'in Nation Court explicitly and emphatically rejected the Crown's site-specific approach, which would have limited Aboriginal title to specific sites of intensive use. The Court held that territorial, semi-nomadic use can establish title over entire territories.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Tsilhqot'in Nation v British Columbia, 2014 SCC 44, Aboriginal title is established over territory (not merely specific sites) where the claimant can demonstrate: (1) sufficient occupation — whether intensive or through semi-nomadic patterns of use — prior to Crown sovereignty; (2) continuity of occupation between pre-sovereignty use and present-day rights; and (3) exclusive occupation, meaning the group treated the land as its own and excluded others. The Court rejected the Crown's site-specific approach: semi-nomadic, territorial use of vast tracts of land can establish title over the entire territory, not just the specific sites of intensive use. Once title is established, the Crown must justify any infringement under the Sparrow framework.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Aboriginal title is not limited to lands covered by treaties. Where no treaties were made, Aboriginal peoples may establish title over their traditional territories through litigation, as the Tsilhqot'in Nation did. Large portions of British Columbia and other provinces were never covered by treaties.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Aboriginal title does not give First Nations absolute immunity from Crown infringement. Tsilhqot'in Nation confirmed that while the Crown must obtain consent or justify infringement, justification is possible where the Crown can demonstrate a compelling and substantial objective that is consistent with the Crown's fiduciary duty. The Sparrow framework — modified for title — applies to infringement of Aboriginal title lands.

tested_concepts: Tsilhqot'in three-part Aboriginal title test
territorial use sufficient — site-specific approach rejected
Sparrow framework applies to Aboriginal title infringement
